1→# Review: deepmind/729.lean
     2→
     3→## 1. Copyright Header
     4→- [x] File begins with the exact copyright block (no leading blank line).
     5→- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.
     6→
     7→## 2. Import Statement
     8→- [x] Exactly one import line (`import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`), immediately after the copyright block, separated by one blank line.
     9→- [x] No other imports are present.
    10→
    11→## 3. Module Docstring
    12→- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
    13→- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 729`.
    14→- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/729`.
    15→- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.
    16→- [x] Bibliography entry present: `[EGRS75]`.
    17→
    18→## 4. Namespace
    19→- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos729 ... end Erdos729`.
    20→- [x] Namespace name uses UpperCamelCase (`Erdos729`).
    21→- [x] No `open` statements needed; all Mathlib names are fully qualified.
    22→
    23→## 5. Custom Definitions
    24→- [x] `DenomPSmooth` has a LaTeX docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
    25→- [x] No `noncomputable` needed (def returns `Prop`).
    26→- [x] No `sorry` in the definition.
    27→- [x] `DenomPSmooth` uses UpperCamelCase (correct for a Prop-valued definition).
    28→- [x] No `local notation` needed.
    29→
    30→## 6. Theorem Statements
    31→- [x] Uses `theorem` keyword.
    32→- [x] Main theorem named `erdos_729` (snake_case).
    33→- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the same attribute line.
    34→- [x] Has a docstring referencing source paper `[EGRS75]`.
    35→
    36→### `answer()` usage
    37→- [ ] **FAIL — should use Case 1 (`answer(True) ↔ P`), not Case 3 (direct assertion).** The module docstring says *"This problem asks whether the same bound holds..."* — the phrase "asks whether" maps to a yes/no question per the checklist. Since the problem has been solved affirmatively ("The answer is yes"), the correct form is:
    38→  ```lean
    39→  theorem erdos_729 : answer(True) ↔
    40→      ∀ (C : ℝ), C > 0 → ∀ (P : ℕ), Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
    41→        (t.1 : ℝ) + (t.2.1 : ℝ) > (t.2.2 : ℝ) + C * Real.log (t.2.2 : ℝ) ∧
    42→        DenomPSmooth t.1 t.2.1 t.2.2 P} := by sorry
    43→  ```
    44→  Currently the theorem is stated as a direct assertion (Case 3) with `(C : ℝ) (hC : C > 0) (P : ℕ)` as theorem parameters.
    45→- [ ] **FAIL — quantifiers are theorem parameters instead of after `↔`.** `(C : ℝ)`, `(hC : C > 0)`, and `(P : ℕ)` should be universally quantified on the RHS of `answer(True) ↔ ...`, not as implicit arguments to the theorem. The linter `linter.style.answer_attribute` would enforce this once `answer()` is introduced.
    46→
    47→## 7. Category Attribute
    48→- [x] Exactly one `@[category research solved]`.
    49→- [x] `research solved` is a valid category value.
    50→- [x] `research solved` with `sorry` in the proof body is acceptable (solved but not formally verified).
    51→
    52→## 8. AMS Attribute
    53→- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS 11]` attribute on the same line as category.
    54→- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed (11 — Number theory).
    55→- [x] Ordering trivially satisfied (single code).
    56→- [x] No duplicate numbers.
    57→- [x] 11 is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.
    58→
    59→## 9. Proof Bodies
    60→- [x] Uses multi-line `by` / `sorry` form (acceptable).
    61→- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
    62→- [x] No informal proof sketches for open problems (problem is solved).
    63→- [x] No `native_decide`.
    64→
    65→## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems
    66→- N/A — no variants present.
    67→
    68→## 11. Code Quality
    69→- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
    70→- [x] Unicode math symbols used (`ℕ`, `ℚ`, `ℝ`, `∀`, `∣`, `≤`, `×`, `∧`).
    71→- [x] No debug code.
    72→- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
    73→- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
    74→- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.
    75→
    76→## 12. Build Verification
    77→- [ ] Not verified (requires `lake build`).
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→## Summary
    82→
    83→**Two issues found, both related to `answer()` usage:**
    84→
    85→1. The problem is phrased as a yes/no question ("asks whether the same bound holds") and has been solved affirmatively, so it should use `answer(True) ↔ P` (Case 1), not a direct assertion (Case 3).
    86→2. As a consequence, the universally quantified variables `C`, `hC`, and `P` should move from theorem parameters to the RHS of the `↔` (i.e., `answer(True) ↔ ∀ C, C > 0 → ∀ P, ...`).
    87→
    88→Everything else — copyright header, import, docstrings, namespace, definition style, category/AMS attributes, proof body, and code quality — conforms to the checklist.
    89→

file.content:
# Review: deepmind/729.lean

## 1. Copyright Header
- [x] File begins with the exact copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement
- [x] Exactly one import line (`import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`), immediately after the copyright block, separated by one blank line.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring
- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 729`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/729`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.
- [x] Bibliography entry present: `[EGRS75]`.

## 4. Namespace
- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos729 ... end Erdos729`.
- [x] Namespace name uses UpperCamelCase (`Erdos729`).
- [x] No `open` statements needed; all Mathlib names are fully qualified.

## 5. Custom Definitions
- [x] `DenomPSmooth` has a LaTeX docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [x] No `noncomputable` needed (def returns `Prop`).
- [x] No `sorry` in the definition.
- [x] `DenomPSmooth` uses UpperCamelCase (correct for a Prop-valued definition).
- [x] No `local notation` needed.

## 6. Theorem Statements
- [x] Uses `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem named `erdos_729` (snake_case).
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the same attribute line.
- [x] Has a docstring referencing source paper `[EGRS75]`.

### `answer()` usage
- [ ] **FAIL — should use Case 1 (`answer(True) ↔ P`), not Case 3 (direct assertion).** The module docstring says *"This problem asks whether the same bound holds..."* — the phrase "asks whether" maps to a yes/no question per the checklist. Since the problem has been solved affirmatively ("The answer is yes"), the correct form is:
  ```lean
  theorem erdos_729 : answer(True) ↔
      ∀ (C : ℝ), C > 0 → ∀ (P : ℕ), Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
        (t.1 : ℝ) + (t.2.1 : ℝ) > (t.2.2 : ℝ) + C * Real.log (t.2.2 : ℝ) ∧
        DenomPSmooth t.1 t.2.1 t.2.2 P} := by sorry
  ```
  Currently the theorem is stated as a direct assertion (Case 3) with `(C : ℝ) (hC : C > 0) (P : ℕ)` as theorem parameters.
- [ ] **FAIL — quantifiers are theorem parameters instead of after `↔`.** `(C : ℝ)`, `(hC : C > 0)`, and `(P : ℕ)` should be universally quantified on the RHS of `answer(True) ↔ ...`, not as implicit arguments to the theorem. The linter `linter.style.answer_attribute` would enforce this once `answer()` is introduced.

## 7. Category Attribute
- [x] Exactly one `@[category research solved]`.
- [x] `research solved` is a valid category value.
- [x] `research solved` with `sorry` in the proof body is acceptable (solved but not formally verified).

## 8. AMS Attribute
- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS 11]` attribute on the same line as category.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed (11 — Number theory).
- [x] Ordering trivially satisfied (single code).
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] 11 is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.

## 9. Proof Bodies
- [x] Uses multi-line `by` / `sorry` form (acceptable).
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
- [x] No informal proof sketches for open problems (problem is solved).
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems
- N/A — no variants present.

## 11. Code Quality
- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used (`ℕ`, `ℚ`, `ℝ`, `∀`, `∣`, `≤`, `×`, `∧`).
- [x] No debug code.
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification
- [ ] Not verified (requires `lake build`).

---

## Summary

**Two issues found, both related to `answer()` usage:**

1. The problem is phrased as a yes/no question ("asks whether the same bound holds") and has been solved affirmatively, so it should use `answer(True) ↔ P` (Case 1), not a direct assertion (Case 3).
2. As a consequence, the universally quantified variables `C`, `hC`, and `P` should move from theorem parameters to the RHS of the `↔` (i.e., `answer(True) ↔ ∀ C, C > 0 → ∀ P, ...`).

Everything else — copyright header, import, docstrings, namespace, definition style, category/AMS attributes, proof body, and code quality — conforms to the checklist.

content.text: The review failed with two issues related to `answer()` usage. Let me read the current file and the checklist for reference.

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 729
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/729](https://www.erdosproblems.com/729)
    23→
    24→Erdős proved that if $a! b! \mid n!$ then $a + b \le n + O(\log n)$. This problem asks
    25→whether the same bound holds when divisibility is only required "up to small
    26→primes," i.e., the denominator of $n!/(a! b!)$ is supported only on bounded primes.
    27→
    28→The answer is yes (the bound still holds). Proved by Barreto and Leeham.
    29→
    30→[EGRS75] Erdős, P., Graham, R., Ruzsa, I. Z., and Straus, E. G.,
    31→_On the prime factors of $\binom{2n}{n}$_, Math. Comp. **29** (1975), 83–92.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos729
    35→
    36→/-- The denominator of $n!/(a! \cdot b!)$, when written in lowest terms, is $P$-smooth:
    37→all its prime factors are at most $P$. -/
    38→def DenomPSmooth (a b n P : ℕ) : Prop :=
    39→  ∀ p : ℕ, Nat.Prime p →
    40→    p ∣ ((n.factorial : ℚ) / ((a.factorial : ℚ) * (b.factorial : ℚ))).den → p ≤ P
    41→

    43→Erdős Problem 729 [EGRS75]:
    44→

    46→$a + b > n + C \cdot \log n$ and the denominator of $n!/(a! b!)$ is $P$-smooth, is finite.
    47→-/
    48→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    49→theorem erdos_729 (C : ℝ) (hC : C > 0) (P : ℕ) :
    50→    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
    51→      (t.1 : ℝ) + (t.2.1 : ℝ) > (t.2.2 : ℝ) + C * Real.log (t.2.2 : ℝ) ∧

file.content:
/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 729

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/729](https://www.erdosproblems.com/729)

Erdős proved that if $a! b! \mid n!$ then $a + b \le n + O(\log n)$. This problem asks
whether the same bound holds when divisibility is only required "up to small
primes," i.e., the denominator of $n!/(a! b!)$ is supported only on bounded primes.

The answer is yes (the bound still holds). Proved by Barreto and Leeham.

[EGRS75] Erdős, P., Graham, R., Ruzsa, I. Z., and Straus, E. G.,
_On the prime factors of $\binom{2n}{n}$_, Math. Comp. **29** (1975), 83–92.
-/

namespace Erdos729

/-- The denominator of $n!/(a! \cdot b!)$, when written in lowest terms, is $P$-smooth:
all its prime factors are at most $P$. -/
def DenomPSmooth (a b n P : ℕ) : Prop :=
  ∀ p : ℕ, Nat.Prime p →
    p ∣ ((n.factorial : ℚ) / ((a.factorial : ℚ) * (b.factorial : ℚ))).den → p ≤ P

/--
Erdős Problem 729 [EGRS75]:

For any $C > 0$ and any prime bound $P$, the set of triples $(a, b, n)$ such that
$a + b > n + C \cdot \log n$ and the denominator of $n!/(a! b!)$ is $P$-smooth, is finite.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_729 (C : ℝ) (hC : C > 0) (P : ℕ) :
    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
      (t.1 : ℝ) + (t.2.1 : ℝ) > (t.2.2 : ℝ) + C * Real.log (t.2.2 : ℝ) ∧
      DenomPSmooth t.1 t.2.1 t.2.2 P} := by
  sorry

end Erdos729

1→# Formal Conjectures PR Checklist
     2→
     3→Every item must pass before a PR is approved. Reviewers: reject if any box is unchecked
     4→without an explicit justification.
     5→
     6→Sources: [`formal-conjectures/README.md`](../formal-conjectures/README.md) (Style Guidelines §1–7),
     7→[`AGENTS.md`](../formal-conjectures/AGENTS.md), and the five custom linters in
     8→`FormalConjectures/Util/`.
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Copyright Header
    13→
    14→- [ ] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line):
    15→  ```
    16→  /-
    17→  Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
    18→
    19→  Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
    20→  you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
    21→  You may obtain a copy of the License at
    22→
    23→      https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
    24→
    25→  Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    26→  distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    27→  WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    28→  See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    29→  limitations under the License.
    30→  -/
    31→  ```
    32→- [ ] Year is exactly `2026` (the linter accepts any 4-digit year, but new files must use the current year).
    33→
    34→## 2. Import Statement
    35→
    36→- [ ] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line):
    37→  ```
    38→  import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    39→  ```
    40→- [ ] No other imports are present.
    41→
    42→## 3. Module Docstring
    43→
    44→- [ ] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
    45→- [ ] Starts with a heading: `# Erdos Problem N` (or appropriate title).
    46→- [ ] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to the source, e.g.:
    47→  ```
    48→  *Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/N](https://www.erdosproblems.com/N)
    49→  ```
    50→- [ ] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.
    51→- [ ] Optional: bibliography entries for related papers.
    52→
    53→## 4. Namespace
    54→
    55→- [ ] All declarations are inside a namespace (`namespace ErdosN ... end ErdosN`).
    56→  - Linter `linter.style.namespace` enforces this; error: *"The declaration '...' is not inside a namespace."*
    57→- [ ] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (e.g., `Erdos42`, not `erdos_42`).
    58→- [ ] `open` / `open scoped` statements appear **before** the namespace block when opening Mathlib namespaces (e.g., `open Filter Nat Real`).
    59→
    60→## 5. Custom Definitions
    61→
    62→- [ ] Helper `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations have a LaTeX docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
    63→- [ ] Definitions that involve classical choice or nonconstructive operations are marked `noncomputable`.
    64→- [ ] No `sorry` in definitions (only in theorem proof bodies).
    65→- [ ] Types and Props use `UpperCamelCase` (e.g., `IsCoveringSystem`).
    66→- [ ] Functions returning types use `lowerCamelCase` (e.g., `normalizedPrimeGap`).
    67→- [ ] Use `local notation` for problem-specific notation, scoped inside the namespace.
    68→- [ ] Consider adding `@[category test]` or `@[category API]` sanity checks for new definitions to verify they behave as expected.
    69→
    70→## 6. Theorem Statements
    71→
    72→- [ ] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` or `lemma` keyword (not `def` or `example`).
    73→- [ ] Main theorem is named `erdos_N` (snake_case, matching the namespace number).
    74→- [ ] Name follows Lean/Mathlib conventions: `snake_case` for terms of Props.
    75→- [ ] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` attributes on the **same attribute line** (e.g., `@[category research open, AMS 11]`).
    76→- [ ] Main theorems and non-obvious variants have a docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
    77→- [ ] Theorem docstrings should ideally reference the source paper(s) (e.g., `[Er95]`, `[AhKh97]`).
    78→
    79→### `answer()` usage
    80→
    81→The rule depends on how the **original problem is phrased in English**, not how the
    82→Lean code currently looks. There are three cases (per `formal-conjectures` README §6
    83→and AGENTS.md):
    84→
    85→**Case 1 — Problem asks a yes/no question** ("Does ...", "Are there ...", "Is it true that ...", "Can ...", "asked whether ..."):
    86→
    87→- [ ] The theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P`, where `P` formalizes the question as asked:
    88→  ```lean
    89→  /-- English: "Does P hold?" -/
    90→  theorem erdos_N : answer(sorry) ↔ P := by sorry
    91→  ```
    92→- [ ] If the problem has been solved, replace `answer(sorry)` with `answer(True)` or `answer(False)` accordingly:
    93→  ```lean
    94→  -- Question answered affirmatively:
    95→  theorem erdos_N : answer(True) ↔ P := by sorry
    96→  -- Question answered negatively:
    97→  theorem erdos_N : answer(False) ↔ P := by sorry
    98→  ```
    99→- [ ] The RHS (`P`) formalizes the **question as originally asked**, so that `answer(True)` means "yes" and `answer(False)` means "no" to that question.
   100→

file.content:
# Formal Conjectures PR Checklist

Every item must pass before a PR is approved. Reviewers: reject if any box is unchecked
without an explicit justification.

Sources: [`formal-conjectures/README.md`](../formal-conjectures/README.md) (Style Guidelines §1–7),
[`AGENTS.md`](../formal-conjectures/AGENTS.md), and the five custom linters in
`FormalConjectures/Util/`.

---

## 1. Copyright Header

- [ ] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line):
  ```
  /-
  Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

  Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
  you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
  You may obtain a copy of the License at

      https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

  Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
  distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
  WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
  See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
  limitations under the License.
  -/
  ```
- [ ] Year is exactly `2026` (the linter accepts any 4-digit year, but new files must use the current year).

## 2. Import Statement

- [ ] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line):
  ```
  import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
  ```
- [ ] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [ ] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [ ] Starts with a heading: `# Erdos Problem N` (or appropriate title).
- [ ] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to the source, e.g.:
  ```
  *Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/N](https://www.erdosproblems.com/N)
  ```
- [ ] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.
- [ ] Optional: bibliography entries for related papers.

## 4. Namespace

- [ ] All declarations are inside a namespace (`namespace ErdosN ... end ErdosN`).
  - Linter `linter.style.namespace` enforces this; error: *"The declaration '...' is not inside a namespace."*
- [ ] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (e.g., `Erdos42`, not `erdos_42`).
- [ ] `open` / `open scoped` statements appear **before** the namespace block when opening Mathlib namespaces (e.g., `open Filter Nat Real`).

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [ ] Helper `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations have a LaTeX docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [ ] Definitions that involve classical choice or nonconstructive operations are marked `noncomputable`.
- [ ] No `sorry` in definitions (only in theorem proof bodies).
- [ ] Types and Props use `UpperCamelCase` (e.g., `IsCoveringSystem`).
- [ ] Functions returning types use `lowerCamelCase` (e.g., `normalizedPrimeGap`).
- [ ] Use `local notation` for problem-specific notation, scoped inside the namespace.
- [ ] Consider adding `@[category test]` or `@[category API]` sanity checks for new definitions to verify they behave as expected.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [ ] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` or `lemma` keyword (not `def` or `example`).
- [ ] Main theorem is named `erdos_N` (snake_case, matching the namespace number).
- [ ] Name follows Lean/Mathlib conventions: `snake_case` for terms of Props.
- [ ] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` attributes on the **same attribute line** (e.g., `@[category research open, AMS 11]`).
- [ ] Main theorems and non-obvious variants have a docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [ ] Theorem docstrings should ideally reference the source paper(s) (e.g., `[Er95]`, `[AhKh97]`).

### `answer()` usage

The rule depends on how the **original problem is phrased in English**, not how the
Lean code currently looks. There are three cases (per `formal-conjectures` README §6
and AGENTS.md):

**Case 1 — Problem asks a yes/no question** ("Does ...", "Are there ...", "Is it true that ...", "Can ...", "asked whether ..."):

- [ ] The theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P`, where `P` formalizes the question as asked:
  ```lean
  /-- English: "Does P hold?" -/
  theorem erdos_N : answer(sorry) ↔ P := by sorry
  ```
- [ ] If the problem has been solved, replace `answer(sorry)` with `answer(True)` or `answer(False)` accordingly:
  ```lean
  -- Question answered affirmatively:
  theorem erdos_N : answer(True) ↔ P := by sorry
  -- Question answered negatively:
  theorem erdos_N : answer(False) ↔ P := by sorry
  ```
- [ ] The RHS (`P`) formalizes the **question as originally asked**, so that `answer(True)` means "yes" and `answer(False)` means "no" to that question.

   114→

If it asks for a specific value — "what is", "find the minimum", "determine" — use Case 2. If it asserts a fact — "prove that", "show that", a direct inequality or bound — use Case 3.

file.content:
**Case 2 — Problem asks for a specific value** ("What is ...", "Find ...", "Determine ..."):

- [ ] The theorem type uses `= answer(sorry)` (or `answer(sorry) =`), where the answer is a concrete value, not `True`/`False`:
  ```lean
  /-- English: "What is the chromatic number of G?" -/
  theorem problem_N : G.chromaticNumber = answer(sorry) := by sorry
  ```
  ```lean
  /-- English: "Which natural numbers satisfy P?" -/
  theorem problem_N : {n : ℕ | P n} = answer(sorry) := by sorry
  ```
- [ ] When solved, replace `answer(sorry)` with the concrete answer value (e.g., `answer(7)`).
- [ ] Note: providing a trivial or tautological answer (e.g., `{n : ℕ | P n}` itself) does not constitute solving the problem. Whether an answer is mathematically meaningful is outside the scope of the repository.

**Case 3 — Problem is stated as a direct assertion** ("P holds", "Show that ...", "Prove that ..."):

- [ ] The theorem type is `P` directly (no `answer()` wrapper):
  ```lean
  /-- English: "P holds" -/
  theorem erdos_N : P := by sorry
  ```
- [ ] If the problem has been solved to the negative, replace `P` with `¬ P`:
  ```lean
  theorem erdos_N : ¬ P := by sorry
  ```

**Common rules for all cases with `answer()`:**

- [ ] Quantifiers go **after** `answer(sorry)`, not as theorem parameters:
  - Correct: `theorem erdos_N : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ n, P n`
  - Wrong: `theorem erdos_N (n : ℕ) : answer(sorry) ↔ P n`
  - Linter `linter.style.answer_attribute` enforces this.

**How to decide which case applies:** Read the problem's English description (module docstring, theorem docstring, or source reference). If it asks a yes/no question — "asked whether", "is it true that", "does there exist", "can one" — use Case 1. If it asks for a specific value — "what is", "find the minimum", "determine" — use Case 2. If it asserts a fact — "prove that", "show that", a direct inequality or bound — use Case 3.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [ ] Every `theorem`, `lemma`, and `example` has **exactly one** `@[category ...]`.
  - Linter `linter.style.category_attribute` enforces this.
  - Missing: *"Missing problem category attribute"*
  - Duplicate: *"Duplicate category attribute. There should be only one category attribute per declaration"*
- [ ] The value is one of the following (no others are valid):
  | Category | When to use |
  |---|---|
  | `high_school` | Solvable at high-school level |
  | `undergraduate` | Undergraduate-level |
  | `graduate` | Graduate-level |
  | `research open` | Open research problem |
  | `research solved` | Solved but not formally verified |

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 729

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/729](https://www.erdosproblems.com/729)

Erdős proved that if $a! b! \mid n!$ then $a + b \le n + O(\log n)$. This problem asks
whether the same bound holds when divisibility is only required "up to small
primes," i.e., the denominator of $n!/(a! b!)$ is supported only on bounded primes.

The answer is yes (the bound still holds). Proved by Barreto and Leeham.

[EGRS75] Erdős, P., Graham, R., Ruzsa, I. Z., and Straus, E. G.,
_On the prime factors of $\binom{2n}{n}$_, Math. Comp. **29** (1975), 83–92.
-/

namespace Erdos729

/-- The denominator of $n!/(a! \cdot b!)$, when written in lowest terms, is $P$-smooth:
all its prime factors are at most $P$. -/
def DenomPSmooth (a b n P : ℕ) : Prop :=
  ∀ p : ℕ, Nat.Prime p →
    p ∣ ((n.factorial : ℚ) / ((a.factorial : ℚ) * (b.factorial : ℚ))).den → p ≤ P

/--
Erdős Problem 729 [EGRS75]:

For any $C > 0$ and any prime bound $P$, the set of triples $(a, b, n)$ such that
$a + b > n + C \cdot \log n$ and the denominator of $n!/(a! b!)$ is $P$-smooth, is finite.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_729 (C : ℝ) (hC : C > 0) (P : ℕ) :
    Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
      (t.1 : ℝ) + (t.2.1 : ℝ) > (t.2.2 : ℝ) + C * Real.log (t.2.2 : ℝ) ∧
      DenomPSmooth t.1 t.2.1 t.2.2 P} := by
  sorry

end Erdos729

lines: $a + b > n + C \cdot \log n$ and the denominator of $n!/(a! b!)$ is $P$-smooth, is finite.
+    ∀ (C : ℝ), C > 0 → ∀ (P : ℕ), Set.Finite {t : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ |
DenomPSmooth t.1 t.2.1 t.2.2 P} := by

content.text: Fixed. The theorem now uses `answer(True) ↔ ...` (Case 1) with the quantifiers `∀ (C : ℝ), C > 0 → ∀ (P : ℕ), ...` on the RHS of the `↔`, instead of having them as theorem parameters with a direct assertion.
